original_prompt: Please create a new 1 hour long event 'Follow-up meeting' on 2024-05-19 at 10:00 or 16:00 if at 10:00 I already have something. The description should be 'Follow-up meeting to discuss the project.'. The event should have the same participants as 'Introductory meeting' event on 2024-05-15.
assessment_reasoning: The prompt clearly preserves the correct first-tool path: (1) check events on 2024-05-19 to see whether 10:00 is free (get_day_calendar_events) and (2) find the 'Introductory meeting' on 2024-05-15 to copy its participants (search_calendar_events). It also specifies title, duration (1 hour), description, and the fallback time (16:00). Remaining operational uncertainties: time zone and exact datetime format, how to detect a conflict at 10:00 (any overlap vs exact-start collision), what to do if both 10:00 and 16:00 are busy, and what to do if the referenced 'Introductory meeting' is not found or has no participants. These do not change the first-tool choice but leave several execution details open. Calibration adjusted the score from 3 to 5 based on preserved specificity cues.

quoted: Follow-up meeting to discuss the project
exposure_request: Please create a new 1 hour long event 'Follow-up meeting' on 2024-05-19 at 10:00 or 16:00 if at 10:00 I already have something.
core_refs: Follow-up meeting to discuss the project

natural_level_plan.rationale: The prompt naturally supports four distinct ambiguity levels along three real information axes present in the original text: (A) whether the referenced source event ('Introductory meeting') includes its date, (B) how explicitly the target day/time is specified (day cue only vs exact start+end), and (C) how explicit the fallback rule and duration/start-end times are. Each higher level restores one or more of these explicit details while preserving the same first tool choice (a day-level calendar lookup for 2024-05-19). The original prompt corresponds to the most explicit level.

levels.description: Minimal but sufficient: requests a 1-hour 'Follow-up meeting' on 2024-05-19 at 10:00 with a 16:00 fallback; includes exact title and description and asks to copy participants from 'Introductory meeting' (no date given). This is enough to trigger a day lookup for 2024-05-19 but leaves which 'Introductory meeting' ambiguous.
must_include: Description: 'Follow-up meeting to discuss the project.'
Copy participants from 'Introductory meeting' (referenced by title only)

levels.description: Adds the referenced-event date to disambiguate which meeting to copy participants from (Introductory meeting on 2024-05-15) while still omitting exact end-times and timezone. Still clearly points to checking 2024-05-19 first.
must_include: Description: 'Follow-up meeting to discuss the project.'

natural_levels.N1: Please create a new 1 hour event titled 'Follow-up meeting' at 10:00 — if I already have something at 10:00, put it at 16:00. Description: 'Follow-up meeting to discuss the project.' Copy the participants from the 'Introductory meeting'.
natural_levels.N2: Please create a new 1 hour long event 'Follow-up meeting' on 2024-05-19 at 10:00 or 16:00 if at 10:00 I already have something. The description should be 'Follow-up meeting to discuss the project.'. The event should have the same participants as 'Introductory meeting' event on 2024-05-15.

N1.reasoning: The request clearly identifies the event title, duration, desired start times (10:00 with 16:00 fallback), description, and that participants should be copied from the event named 'Introductory meeting' — so a competent agent would first search the calendar for the 'Introductory meeting' to copy participants and then check availability at the requested time. However the prompt omits the date (and timezone) for the new event, which is critical for deciding which day to check for a 10:00 conflict and which occurrence of 'Introductory meeting' to copy from if there are multiple. It also leaves open details like which calendar/account to use, how to handle recurring or all-day conflicts, and which occurrence of a named event to copy from. These omissions preserve the correct first tool family (search/get events) but leave many important execution choices unspecified.

N2.reasoning: The prompt preserves the correct first tool path: check availability on 2024-05-19 (to see if 10:00 is free), retrieve participants from the 'Introductory meeting' on 2024-05-15, then create a 1-hour event at 10:00 or fallback to 16:00. Title, date, duration, description, and the source event for participants are all specified. Remaining minor uncertainties: exact definition of “already have something” (any overlap vs strict start-time conflict), what to do if both proposed slots are busy, and timezone/location/reminder preferences are unspecified but do not change the first-tool choice. Calibration adjusted the score from 4 to 5 based on preserved specificity cues.
